verses.commentary: Adam made love to his wife Eve and she conceived and gave birth to Cain (Qayin, קַיִן). She said, With the help of the LORD I have brought forth a man. Eve recognizes God's role in life-giving. The name Cain sounds like the Hebrew for brought forth or acquired. Life continues despite the fall.

Later she gave birth to his brother Abel (Hevel, הֶבֶל). The name means breath or vapor, suggesting transience or fragility. This foreshadows his fate. Abel kept flocks and Cain worked the soil. Different vocations, both legitimate. The brothers represent pastoralist and agriculturalist ways of life.

In the course of time Cain brought some of the fruits of the soil as an offering to the LORD. Worship is instinctive. Cain offers from his labor. The text notes some of the fruits, perhaps suggesting a casual offering, not the best.

But Abel brought fat portions from some of the firstborn of his flock. Abel offers the best, the firstborn and the fat portions. The LORD looked with favor on Abel and his offering. God's acceptance is mysterious but may relate to the quality and heart behind the gift.

But on Cain and his offering he did not look with favor. Rejection without explicit reason provokes Cain. So Cain was very angry and his face was downcast. Anger and shame. Comparison breeds resentment. Worship becomes competition.
The LORD said to Cain, Why are you angry? Why is your face downcast? God engages Cain, asking questions to prompt self-examination. God does not abandon Cain in his anger.

If you do what is right, will you not be accepted? The path forward is clear: do right. But if you do not do what is right, sin is crouching at your door. Sin is personified as a predator waiting to pounce. It desires to have you but you must rule over it. The same language of desire and rule from 3:16. Cain must master sin, not be mastered by it. This is warning and empowerment.

Cain said to his brother Abel, Let's go out to the field. Premeditation. While they were in the field, Cain attacked his brother Abel and killed him. The first murder. Brother kills brother. Violence escalates from eating forbidden fruit to taking human life. Abel's name (breath, vapor) proves prophetic. Life is fragile.

The LORD said to Cain, Where is your brother Abel? Echoes Where are you? (3:9). God's question invites confession. I don't know, Cain lies. Am I my brother's keeper? Defiance and deflection. The answer, unspoken, is yes. We are responsible for one another.

What have you done? The question exposes the horror. Your brother's blood cries out to me from the ground. Blood has a voice. Innocent blood demands justice. The ground that received Abel's blood will testify against Cain.

Now you are under a curse and driven from the ground which opened its mouth to receive your brother's blood from your hand. The ground, Cain's domain as a farmer, turns against him. Curse echoes the serpent's curse (3:14) and the ground's curse (3:17).

When you work the ground it will no longer yield its crops for you. His vocation is stripped of meaning. You will be a restless wanderer on the earth. Exile and rootlessness. Cain loses home, vocation, stability.
Cain said to the LORD, My punishment is more than I can bear. He laments but does not repent. He feels the weight of consequence but not the guilt of sin.

Today you are driving me from the land and I will be hidden from your presence. Separation from God is the deepest loss. I will be a restless wanderer and whoever finds me will kill me. Cain fears retribution. He knows the law of blood vengeance even before it is codified.

The LORD said, Not so; anyone who kills Cain will suffer vengeance seven times over. God protects even the murderer. Mercy interrupts the cycle of violence. The LORD put a mark on Cain so that no one who found him would kill him. The mark is protection, not shame. Grace extends even to the guilty.

Cain went out from the LORD's presence and lived in the land of Nod, east of Eden. Nod means wandering. Cain settles in exile. East of Eden echoes the direction of expulsion (3:24). Moving further from paradise.

Cain made love to his wife and she conceived and gave birth to Enoch. Life continues even in exile. Cain was building a city and named it after his son Enoch. Civilization begins. The wanderer builds a city, seeking permanence. Culture emerges from brokenness.

To Enoch was born Irad, and Irad was the father of Mehujael, and Mehujael was the father of Methushael, and Methushael was the father of Lamech. Genealogy establishes continuity. Cain's line develops.
Lamech married two women, one named Adah and the other Zillah. Polygamy enters. Departure from the one flesh union of Genesis 2. Fracture multiplies.
Adah gave birth to Jabal; he was the father of those who live in tents and raise livestock. Pastoral nomadism. Cultural development, specialization of labor.
His brother's name was Jubal; he was the father of all who play stringed instruments and pipes. Music and the arts. Beauty emerges alongside violence. Humans create culture even in a fallen world.

Zillah gave birth to Tubal-Cain, who forged all kinds of tools out of bronze and iron. Technology, metallurgy, tool-making. Human ingenuity advances. Tubal-Cain's sister was Naamah. She is named, suggesting significance, though her role is unstated.

Lamech said to his wives, Adah and Zillah, listen to me; wives of Lamech, hear my words. I have killed a man for wounding me, a young man for injuring me. Lamech's song is a boast of violence. Retribution escalates. Where Cain killed once, Lamech kills for mere injury.

If Cain is avenged seven times, then Lamech seventy-seven times. He perverts God's protection of Cain into a claim of personal invincibility. Violence and pride compound. Civilization advances but so does sin.

Adam made love to his wife again and she gave birth to a son and named him Seth (Shet, שֵׁת), saying, God has granted me another child in place of Abel, since Cain killed him. Seth means granted or appointed. A new beginning. Hope after loss.

Seth also had a son and named him Enosh. At that time people began to call on the name of the LORD. Worship is renewed. Calling on God's name suggests prayer, invocation, covenant relationship. Despite the spread of sin, a line of faithfulness emerges.
